Commas would be correct if it was an adverbial that is movable around the sentence (like "of course", "according to Jerry", "that we can tell from this story"). Em dash is more radical than comma. Both are equally correct but for different occasions. Em dash follows the format "IC -- thought -- IC".

text: * The Third Rule: Experiences don't lead to mistakes, but they lead to lessons learned.
correct: * The Third Rule: Experiences don't lead to mistakes, but they lead to lessons learned. You can also say this without the comma and verb like: Experiences don't lead to mistakes but to lessons learned. Commas with conjunctions separate two independent clauses (like this sentence: take out ",but" and it is two complete sentences). But if the two clauses have the same verb like "lead", you can just shorten the second clause like this so that it goes into the first. So lead refers to not making mistakes but also to the lessons learned.

text: Today, I started reading a book called; How to forgive even when you can't forget, written by Thomas Herold.
correct: Today, I started reading a book called How to Forgive Even When You Can't Forget, written by Thomas Herold. Semi-colon should only be used for two reasons: 1) connecting two independent clauses such as "He went back to the grocery store; milk is what he forgot." 2) acting as a comma if commas are part of things in a list (called a "super comma") such as "This summer, I will visit Barcelona, Spain; Paris, France; and Milan, Italy." In that case it is just part of title (the city, country) so to avoid confusion one uses semi-colon. The funny thing is that most English writers do not know how to use the semi-colon (or em dash "--" for that matter), but you do now!

text: I call him professor Okay, because he seems not capable of completing one sentence without using the word okay 2 or 3 times.
correct: I call him Professor Okay, because he seems incapable of completing a sentence without saying the word "okay" at least two or three times. For proper names like Doctor Jones or Professor Smith or whatever, you capitalize their full assumed title. Usually they have earned the title... like with a PhD or by working at a university, but it also works in jokes, as well, by analogy. Especially if he prefers his real title like Professor Smith or whatever his real name is. Just because it is like a nickname... and nicknames would be capitalized, because it is technically something you might address them as (even if not to their face).

Many schools have "summer break", with no school sessions in July and August. Other English-speaking countries use "holiday" like Americans use "vacation", which is the sense you have here. Comment: In English, we usually "learn about" things rather than "get to know" things. The only exception I can think of at the moment is when the object is a person. Then either works, but they mean slightly different things. When you "get to know" a person, then you are personally familiar with them. text: Today, it was very cold and strong windy day in Tokyo.
correct: Today, it was very cold and windy day in Tokyo. When you put a -y at the end of weather words (wind-y, snow-y, storm-y, etc.) the words become an adjective; a word used to describe nouns. Strong is also an adjective. You want to say the wind was strong but, when you put one adjective in front of another "strong windy" they both describe the next noun ---> day. What happens is you have a cold day in Tokyo. You have a windy day in Tokyo. Finally you have a strong day in Tokyo!? If you want to talk about how windy the day was you can use the word very to talk about how the windy the wind was. It was very cold and there was a very strong wind today in Tokyo!

text: In other words,`My funny lover`?

Originally, the phrase meant something like "my friend Valentine is (a little) funny, but I like him". According to a Wikipedia page: "In the original play, a character named Billie Smith (played by Mitzi Green) sings the song to Valentine "Val" LaMar (played by Ray Heatherton). From Wordnet http://wordnetweb.princeton.edu/perl/webwn?s=valentine [1. valentine (a sweetheart chosen to receive a greeting on Saint Valentine's Day) "will you be my valentine?" 2. Valentine (a card sent or given (as to a sweetheart) on Saint Valentine's Day)]. So to confirm your way of reading it, yes, you can easily imagine a situation when a playful woman (in the West, unlike in Japan it is usually a woman who receives a Valentine card or some Valentine's sweets) says to her friend "you're my funny Valentine" which could be translated to male speech as "I think you are funny, but you are sweet, so you are my piece of chocolate now." or "You're as funny and as sweet as a card for me on Saint Valentine's day.") text: Therefore, I participate in the running club which Nike organized on Tuesdays in order to keep fit .

Moving the time to the front of the sentence makes it read more smoothly. R.e. a/the, because Nike presumably has many different running clubs, you should use a running club and not the running club.
